operator.note: Provocative removal of the entire scalar-verifier-plus-leaderboard substrate. Forces invention of a transfer/recurrence-based success criterion, directly realizing the loosened dof4: progress = recurs under a different incentive structure & generalizes, not = monotone climb on a fixed float.

question: Provocation: the leaderboard does not exist and there is no scalar verifier — results are scored only by whether a *different* cohort of agents, six months later and working on *different* problems, can reuse the traces they wrote. Under this removal, (a) how do we operationally *detect* "progress" at all, and (b) does the resulting metric — cross-problem, cross-cohort transfer of shared traces — rank the same 12 archived results as wins, or a substantially different set?

floor.minimal_experiment: Use the existing M2 archive (12 entries, each a research-problem result with a recorded scalar/judge score and a saved derivation trace) as the held-out 'cohort A' corpus. (1) Freeze cohort-A's scalar ranking as the control. (2) Build a transfer metric T(x): for each of N >= 2 later 'cohort B' agent runs operating on a DIFFERENT problem set, log whether each cohort-A trace x is retrieved-and-cited / copied-into / measurably-shortens-time-to-solution for the cohort-B task. T(x) = fraction of cohort-B problems where x was reused with a downstream benefit above a pre-registered threshold. A cheap deliverable proxy: run two batches of the engine on disjoint problem partitions, expose batch-1 traces to batch-2 agents via the archive, and instrument reuse. (3) Compute Kendall's tau between the scalar ranking and the T-ranking over the 12. Pre-register tau* = 0.6 as the agreement cutoff.
floor.falsifiable_prediction: The provocation's whole motivation is that transfer-based progress diverges from scalar climbing. Falsifiable claim: the transfer ranking will DISAGREE with the scalar ranking, Kendall's tau < 0.6, and at least the top-ranked scalar result will NOT be the top transfer result. If instead tau >= 0.6 (rankings substantially agree), the provocation is refuted — the scalar verifier was already a faithful proxy for transfer and removing it bought nothing.
floor.control: The original scalar/judge ranking of the same 12 archived results (the leaderboard that the PO operator 'removed'). Same items, same archive, only the scoring substrate swapped — so any rank divergence is attributable to the metric, not to a different population.

value_profile.valuable_to_whom: Eval/benchmark designers for autonomous research agents and AI-lab leaderboard maintainers (who fear scalar-target gaming); quality-diversity / MAP-Elites researchers needing a fitness proxy when no ground-truth verifier exists; and the frameshift engine itself, which currently relies on un-calibrated Claude self-scores.

prior_art.known_name: Goodhart's law (scalar target ceases to be a good measure once optimized) + downstream-reuse / criterion-validity evaluation (cf. citation- and reuse-based impact metrics in bibliometrics, and 'transfer/downstream-task' eval in ML). The leaderboard-removal framing also echoes 'construct validity' critiques of single-scalar benchmarks.
prior_art.novelty_watermark: Not a new mechanism. This is the operationalization of a well-known idea — replace a gameable scalar with downstream-transfer/recurrence — inside a specific new scene: a quality-diversity search over RESEARCH PROBLEMS where the 'reusers' are a later, differently-incentivized agent cohort on different problems. Novel contribution is the concrete cross-cohort/cross-problem trace-reuse instrument and the rank-agreement test against the scalar control, not the underlying transfer principle.
